title: Systems & the Outside World
subtitle: When tech fails and the world intrudes

hook.metaphor: The kitchen can run perfectly and still go dark — because the power grid failed, or the gas supplier didn't deliver. Some operational risks aren't about your people at all; they're about the **systems** you depend on and the **outside world** you can't control. The modern twist: more of your kitchen is now plugged into someone else's wiring.
hook.scenario: A neobank's app goes down for six hours. The cause? Not their code — a global outage at the cloud provider they rent everything from. Their customers don't care whose fault it is; they just can't reach their money.

learn: The third source — **systems** — is the risk that the technology you run on fails: an outage, a bug, a failed change, a capacity overload. As finance becomes software, systems risk has swollen from a back-office concern to an existential one. When the app is down, the business is down.

**Cyber risk** is the sharp end of systems risk: attackers deliberately breaking, stealing, or holding your systems to ransom. It's so important it's often treated as its own GRC theme (it is, in this academy) — but at root, a breach is an operational loss.

The fourth source — **external events** — is everything outside your walls: natural disasters, pandemics, power cuts, civil disruption. You can't prevent them, so the game changes from *avoidance* to **resilience**: can you keep operating, and recover fast, when they hit?

The biggest modern shift ties systems and external events together: **third-party and concentration risk.** You now depend on cloud providers, payment networks, and SaaS vendors you don't control. When one of them sneezes, thousands of firms catch a cold at once. This is exactly why APRA's **CPS 230** explicitly demands management of service-provider risk and tested business-continuity plans — and why 'it was our vendor's fault' is never an acceptable answer to a regulator or a customer.
bridge: So at work, you can map your hidden dependencies: *what systems and outside parties would take us down if they failed — and do we have a tested plan to keep running without them?* Resilience isn't assuming nothing breaks; it's rehearsing for when it does. The firms that survive outages are the ones that practised the fire drill before the fire.

interactions.title: Whose problem is the outage?
interactions.prompt: The neobank is down because its cloud provider failed. A customer complains. What's the right posture?

options.label: 'Not our fault — blame the cloud provider.'
options.feedback: Outsourcing the work never outsources the responsibility. The customer (and the regulator) hold the bank accountable for resilience, vendor or not.

options.label: Own it, and invest in resilience — backups, failover, a tested continuity plan.
options.feedback: Right. You can't control the vendor's outage, but you can control your dependence on it: redundancy, failover, and a rehearsed plan. That's managing concentration and continuity risk.

options.label: Promise it will never happen again.
options.feedback: You can't promise that — outages will recur. The honest, credible commitment is faster recovery and less single-point dependence, not zero incidents.

interactions.title: Systems, external, or third-party?
interactions.prompt: Sort each scenario. (Some blur together — that's the point of modern operational risk.)

items.label: A bug in your own app crashes checkout
An earthquake closes your data centre
Your database runs out of capacity at peak
A cloud provider you rely on has a global outage

interactions.title: Resilience vocabulary
interactions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.right: Keeping critical operations running through a disruption
The ability to absorb a shock and recover quickly
Over-relying on a single vendor or system
Switching automatically to a backup when the primary fails
Deliberate attacks on your systems and data

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Some operational risk is your tech failing, some is the world outside, and more and more is the vendors you depend on — so the goal isn't 'never break,' it's 'recover fast.'”

checkpoint.q: When a critical cloud vendor's outage takes your service down, who is accountable to your customers and regulator?
options: You — outsourcing the work doesn't outsource the responsibility
Nobody, it's an act of god
checkpoint.explain: You remain responsible for resilience and continuity even when you rely on third parties — a core CPS 230 expectation.

options: Over-relying on a single system, vendor, or point of failure
checkpoint.explain: When too much depends on one provider or system, its failure cascades widely.

checkpoint.q: For external events you can't prevent, the right strategy is…
options: Build and test resilience and business-continuity plans
Buy more of the same single system
checkpoint.explain: You can't stop an earthquake or a grid failure — you rehearse continuity and recovery so you keep running.
